episode_title: Can ParaBros Keep the NFL?
transcript: Paramount used UFC as an example of how this new found synergy between the two is going to work... UFC fights also could be carried on TNT too. 'We do have that flexibility,' he said. Now to me, that was as clear an indication as any that the company, which now basically owns US cable business, plans to splash its various rights around its portfolio to buttress the mining carriage fees.
reason: John Ourand breaks down how Paramount's $110 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery will concretely affect sports distribution, with David Ellison signaling that UFC rights ($7.7B deal) could be spread across both CBS and TNT to maximize carriage fee leverage.

episode_title: Can ParaBros Keep the NFL?
transcript: The NFL on that cost per viewer hour, which arguably is the ultimate driver of value for the networks, is at a significant discount to some other properties, most notably the NBA, which saw these pretty massive step ups several years ago.
reason: Mike Morris reveals that the NFL is actually undervalued relative to the NBA on a cost-per-viewer-hour basis, a key financial metric that underpins the coming NFL rights negotiations and challenges conventional assumptions about the league's $110 billion deal portfolio.

episode_title: Can ParaBros Keep the NFL?
transcript: If I watched the NFL through a PTV subscription... and let's say the NFL is 40% of what's important to me as a subscriber, that's $40 a month... That math does not fit right now. I will tell you that NFL is not getting 40% of the PTV subscriptions... The bundle is the sports tier. Don't get confused that there are people who sign up for the bundle for the entertainment because they are the exception.
reason: Morris provides a striking financial illustration of how the NFL is structurally underpaid relative to its actual value to subscribers, arguing the pay-TV bundle has always been a sports tier in disguise — a framework that justifies significant rights fee increases.

episode_title: Can ParaBros Keep the NFL?
transcript: If there was a business that wants to be in sports media that could potentially do it, ESPN is the only one I wouldn't even be kind of noodling on here about it. But I'm still very skeptical... I think you are relegated to specialty tier instead of mainstream tier if you want to be in the sports business but you don't have NFL content.
reason: Morris delivers a blunt verdict on ESPN's strategic position: even with its dominant college sports portfolio, losing the NFL would relegate ESPN from mainstream to specialty status — a critical insight for understanding why ESPN will pay whatever it takes.

episode_title: Can ParaBros Keep the NFL?
transcript: I think the NFL is very focused on making sure that they continue to explore new partnerships... A global streaming platform probably makes more sense. The other thing I would point out is... when I look at Amazon and YouTube in particular, both with incredibly robust advertising businesses and really what I would consider industry leading next gen advertising businesses with respect to targeting... are you seeing a meaningful increase in the value of the advertising?
reason: Morris makes a specific prediction that YouTube and Netflix will both secure bigger NFL packages than they currently have, and explains how Amazon and Google's superior ad-tech capabilities could justify paying premium prices, reshaping the competitive landscape.

episode_title: Can ParaBros Keep the NFL?
transcript: I would start with Fox. I think Fox is arguably the most solid... The only reason I don't put Paramount and CBS Sports played at that same level is because these structural changes that company has had through the Skydance merger and the acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery. It does open up some potential for change there.
reason: Morris ranks the NFL's Sunday afternoon partners, placing Fox as most secure and flagging Paramount/CBS as slightly more vulnerable due to its ongoing corporate restructuring — a nuanced take on how M&A activity could subtly reshape NFL rights distribution.

episode_title: Can ParaBros Keep the NFL?
transcript: I think Amazon has done a great job with that Thursday night package. I feel like Thursday has almost become fun day. Monday maybe a little diminished... I would argue that the Sunday night package is the most attractive and arguably if bidding opened up you would see the most aggressive bidding on that. Thursday has really come up in stature and maybe that makes it number two whereas Monday night football historically has been that prime time extra window that maybe ends up sliding down.
reason: Morris provides a notable reranking of NFL prime-time windows, suggesting Thursday Night Football on Amazon has surpassed Monday Night Football on ESPN in desirability — a significant shift that could affect ESPN's negotiating position and the relative value of each package.

episode_title: The World’s Cup Is Coming to Trump’s America, with Roger Bennett

There was a broadcaster on ESPN who said, the world's most famous footballer, Charlie Beckham, takes the field. And I screamed at the television, I was like... that moment was like being shown beachfront property that was completely undeveloped.
reason: Bennett reveals the ESPN origin story of Men in Blazers and describes the massive market gap in US soccer media that existed at ESPN, illustrating how underserved the soccer audience was at a major sports network.

episode_title: The World’s Cup Is Coming to Trump’s America, with Roger Bennett

episode_title: The World’s Cup Is Coming to Trump’s America, with Roger Bennett

episode_title: The World’s Cup Is Coming to Trump’s America, with Roger Bennett
transcript: The NFL, obviously, massive, the NBA enormous. But right after that, for this young audience in the United States, it's soccer of the different kinds. We've grown from one podcast to doing over a thousand a year and that's really the growth that's propelling this.
reason: Bennett positions soccer as the third-most important sport for young US audiences behind the NFL and NBA, and reveals the scale of the Men in Blazers operation at over 1,000 podcasts per year — a striking metric for a niche sports media company.

transcript: CBS Paramount, I feel like that's absolutely have to have it... NBC, yes... Fox, it's mission critical. If what is Fox without NFL?... ESPN, I think they're have to... Amazon, I would put them in the need to have it but it's not a game breaker.

episode_title: An NBA Mailbag, LeBron’s Next Move, and the Wild Paramount-WBD Merger With David Jacoby and Matt Belloni
transcript: In a sea where everything is possible, what isn't possible to recreate? And it's known intellectual property. You cannot recreate Batman with AI unless it's licensed... I think that's honestly why Netflix was so interested in buying Warner Bros.

episode_title: An NBA Mailbag, LeBron’s Next Move, and the Wild Paramount-WBD Merger With David Jacoby and Matt Belloni

episode_title: An NBA Mailbag, LeBron’s Next Move, and the Wild Paramount-WBD Merger With David Jacoby and Matt Belloni

episode_title: Inside a Duke Blue Devils Athletics Threat Assessment

episode_title: How David Ellison Plans to Mash Two Major Studios Into One

episode_title: How David Ellison Plans to Mash Two Major Studios Into One
transcript: The deck they provided said that within 24 months, they would bring leverage down to three times. There would be three billion of synergies that they would grow their EBITDA of $12 billion to $14 billion... 12 billion of EBITDA never grew above 12 billion. In fact, it is sub nine billion dollars today.
reason: Greenfield draws a devastating parallel between WBD's failed financial predictions five years ago and the nearly identical projections Ellison is now making — same $12B starting EBITDA, but now promising $6B in synergies instead of $3B. A concrete historical case study for skepticism.

episode_title: How David Ellison Plans to Mash Two Major Studios Into One

episode_title: How David Ellison Plans to Mash Two Major Studios Into One

So where do you, financial analyst, where do you come down on this? Because 79 billion dollars in debt and where they are in EBITDA at 12 billion or so, that doesn't match up.

episode_title: How David Ellison Plans to Mash Two Major Studios Into One

episode_title: How David Ellison Plans to Mash Two Major Studios Into One

episode_title: How David Ellison Plans to Mash Two Major Studios Into One
transcript: Nobody believes Disney is going to evaporate. I think there's plenty of people that believe Paramount on its own or Warner Brothers on its own might evaporate... episode_title: How David Ellison Plans to Mash Two Major Studios Into One

episode_title: The NBA's Shadow Over the NFL

episode_title: The NBA's Shadow Over the NFL

episode_title: The NBA's Shadow Over the NFL
